title: VLANs (Virtual LANs)
description: Logical segmentation of a physical network into multiple isolated broadcast domains.
fullExplanation: A VLAN (Virtual LAN) partitions a physical switch into multiple logical switches. Devices in different VLANs cannot communicate at Layer 2 — they need a router (or Layer 3 switch) to communicate. VLANs are identified by a 12-bit VLAN ID (1-4094) in the 802.1Q tag added to Ethernet frames. Access ports carry traffic for one VLAN (untagged). Trunk ports carry traffic for multiple VLANs (tagged with 802.1Q).

header.size: 4 bytes (802.1Q tag inserted into Ethernet frame)

fields.name: DEI
fields.description: Drop Eligible Indicator — mark frame for dropping under congestion

fields.name: VID
fields.description: VLAN Identifier — 0-4095 (0 and 4095 reserved, 1 is default)

portTypes.description: Carries one VLAN. Frames untagged. Used for end devices (PCs, phones).
Carries multiple VLANs. Frames tagged with 802.1Q. Used between switches and routers.
Untagged VLAN on a trunk port. Default is VLAN 1 — change for security.
Separate VLAN for VoIP traffic — enables QoS prioritization.

keyFacts: VLANs reduce broadcast domains — less broadcast traffic per segment
Inter-VLAN routing requires a router or Layer 3 switch
Router-on-a-stick: single trunk link to router with subinterfaces per VLAN
Native VLAN mismatch: security risk — change native VLAN from default VLAN 1
VLAN 1 is the default — all ports start in VLAN 1 — segregate management traffic
802.1Q double-tagging (VLAN hopping): attacker adds extra tag to jump VLANs
Private VLAN (PVLAN): isolate ports within same VLAN — used in hosting environments
